Title: Osteoarthritis of the Knee Clinical Care Standard
Description: This clinical care standard describes the care that should be received by patients aged 45+ when they have knee pain and may have knee osteoarthritis.
Body: Osteoarthritis is a chronic disease of the synovial joints, resulting in pain accompanied by varying degrees of functional limitation and reduced quality of life.
title: Osteoarthritis of the Knee Clinical Care Standard

content:
This clinical care standard describes the care that should be received by patients aged 45+ when they have knee pain and may have knee osteoarthritis. It covers initial clinical assessment, ongoing conservative management over the course of the condition and referral to surgery, if required.
Main sections:
- Osteoarthritis of the knee clinical care standard
- About the clinical care standards

content: Body: The document outlines a framework for the communication of pathology information within the request-test-report cycle. It sets minimum standards to ensure the integrity of patient information during the transfer of information with external parties
title: Requirements for information communication and reporting v5

content: . To manage potential risks, laboratories must be able to ensure the confidentiality, integrity (including authenticity) and availability (collectively known as information security) of messages received and sent to the authorised recipient. Title: Requirements for the estimation of measurement of uncertainty
Description: This document provides guidance for pathology laboratories on an acceptable degree of variation in measurements.
title: Requirements for the estimation of measurement of uncertainty

content: Body: All types of measurements have some inaccuracies, which means that measurement results can only be estimates of the quantities being measured. Measurement uncertainty provides quantitative estimates of the level of confidence that a laboratory has in the analytical precision of test results, and is an essential component of a quality system for medical laboratories.
title: Requirements for the estimation of measurement of uncertainty

content:
This document provides guidance for pathology laboratories on an acceptable degree of variation in measurements. It provides a diverse range of examples for the different disciplines of pathology to consider when estimating measurement.

content:
This clinical care standard describes the care that should be received by patients aged 16+ who present with low back pain, with or without leg pain. It covers the early clinical assessment, management, and review and referral of people with low back pain symptoms who present with a new acute episode. Description: This clinical care standard describes the care that patients aged 18+ should receive to reduce their risk of developing hospital-acquired VTE both in hospital and following discharge.
title: Venous Thromboembolism Prevention Clinical Care Standard

content:
Body: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) is a disease that can cause complications such as post-thrombotic syndrome, pulmonary hypertension, recurrent thrombosis and death.
This clinical care standard describes the care that patients aged 18+ should receive to reduce their risk of developing hospital-acquired VTE both in hospital and following discharge.

Description: This document sets out the standards for general practices, developed by the Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP).
title: Standards for general practices (5th edition)

content: Body: This document sets out the standards for general practices, developed by the Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP). The purpose of these standards is to protect patients from harm by improving the quality and safety of health services. The standards also support general practices in identifying and addressing any gaps in their systems and processes.
title: Standards for general practices (5th edition)

content:
The indicators in this edition have, where appropriate, been written with a focus on outcomes and patients, instead of on prescribed processes or what the practice does. By focusing on outcomes, practices can develop systems and processes that reflect their preferred ways of working, and they can choose how to demonstrate that they meet the intent of each indicator.

It ensures that data on medication intake and compliance can be accurately shared and interpreted between devices and management applications
title: Health informatics — Device interoperability — Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Medication monitor

title:
Health informatics — Device interoperability
Part 10421: Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Peak expiratory flow monitor (peak flow)

content: Body: This document defines a common core of functionality of a peak-flow monitoring device. The use case is restricted to personal respiratory monitoring and therefore does not include hospital-based spirometry. Continuous and high-acuity monitoring (e.g., for emergency response) are outside the scope of the use case
title:
Health informatics — Device interoperability
Part 10421: Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Peak expiratory flow monitor (peak flow)

content: . In the context of personal health devices, a peak flow meter is a device is used to measure the respiratory function of those managing respiratory conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. The ability to identify declining respiratory status prior to the need for acute intervention improves the quality of life for the individual while reducing the overall costs of care
title:
Health informatics — Device interoperability
Part 10421: Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Peak expiratory flow monitor (peak flow)

content: . Respiratory status data are collected by a personal respiratory monitoring device and forwarded to a central data repository for review and action by a health care provider. The data are episodic in nature and are forwarded at designated intervals or when the person is symptomatic.
title:
Health informatics — Device interoperability

Title: Health informatics – HL7 Personal Health Record System Functional Model, Release 2 (PHR-S FM)
Description: This document defines a standardised model of the functions that may be present in personal health record (PHR) systems.
title: Health informatics – HL7 Personal Health Record System Functional Model, Release 2 (PHR-S FM)

content: Body: This document defines a standardised model of the functions that may be present in personal health record (PHR) systems. This PHR System Functional Model (PHR-S FM) is a generic model for a variety of patient-centric tools that help individuals maintain a longitudinal view of their health history.
title: Health informatics – HL7 Personal Health Record System Functional Model, Release 2 (PHR-S FM)

content:
The information exchange enabled by a PHR-S supports the retrieval and population of clinical documents and summaries, minimum data sets, and other input and outputs.
This Functional Model is not a messaging specification, an implementation specification, a conformance specification, a specification for the underlying PHR (the record itself) or a conformance or conformance testing metric.
title: Health informatics – HL7 Personal Health Record System Functional Model, Release 2 (PHR-S FM)

title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40102: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Capabilities for mitigation

content: Body: This standard defines a security baseline of application-layer cyber security mitigation techniques for certain use cases or for when certain criteria are met
title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40102: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Capabilities for mitigation

content: . It provides a scalable information-security toolbox appropriate for personal health device and point-of-care device interfaces, which fulfils the requirements and recommendations from the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the European Network and Information Security Agency (ENISA).
title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40102: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Capabilities for mitigation

content: This standard maps to the NIST cybersecurity framework [B15]; IEC TR 80001-2-2 [B8]; and the STRIDE classification scheme (spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial of service, and elevation of privilege)
title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40102: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Capabilities for mitigation

content: . The mitigation techniques are based on the extended CIA triad (Clause 4) and are described generally to allow manufacturers to determine the most appropriate algorithms and implementations.

title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40101: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Processes for vulnerability assessment

content: . This iterative vulnerability assessment uses the STRIDE classification scheme (spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial of service, and elevation of privilege) and the embedded Common Vulnerability Scoring System (eCVSS)
title: Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 40101: Foundational — Cybersecurity — Processes for vulnerability assessment

content: . The assessment includes system context, system decomposition, pre-mitigation scoring, mitigation, and post-mitigation scoring and iterates until the remaining vulnerabilities are reduced to an acceptable level of risk.

content: Title: Health informatics — Framework for healthcare and related data reporting
title: Health informatics — Framework for healthcare and related data reporting

content: Description: This standard deals with the reporting of data to support improved public health, more effective health care and better health outcomes. It provides guidance and requirements for those developing or deploying a healthcare data reporting service, addressing data capture, processing, aggregation and data modelling, and architecture and technology approaches.
title: Health informatics — Framework for healthcare and related data reporting

content: Body: This standard deals with the reporting of data to support improved public health, more effective health care and better health outcomes. It provides guidance and requirements for those developing or deploying a healthcare data reporting service, addressing data capture, processing, aggregation and data modelling, and architecture and technology approaches
title: Health informatics — Framework for healthcare and related data reporting

content: . The role of a healthcare data reporting service is to enable data analyses in support of effective policies and decision-making, to improve quality of care, to improve health services organisations and to influence learning and research. This standard has relevance to both developing and more established health systems. It enables meaningful comparison of programs and outcomes.

Description: This standard specifies how medical waveforms, such as electrocardiogram, electroencephalogram, spirometry waveform and so on, are described for interoperability among healthcare information systems.
title: Health informatics — Medical waveform format — Part 1: Encoding rules

content: Body: This standard specifies how medical waveforms, such as electrocardiogram, electroencephalogram, spirometry waveform and so on, are described for interoperability among healthcare information systems. This standard can be used with other relevant protocols, such as HL7, DICOM, the ISO/IEEE 11073 series and database management systems for each purpose
title: Health informatics — Medical waveform format — Part 1: Encoding rules

content: . This is a general specification, so specifications for particular waveform types and for harmonisation with DICOM, SCP-ECG, X73 and so on are not given. This standard does not include lower layer protocols for message exchange. For example, a critical real-time application such as a patient monitoring system is out of scope and is an implementation issue.

Title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Walk-through screening station
Description: This standard specifies the operation of a walk-through screening station (WTSS) for mass testing as part of pandemic response management (for example, during the COVID-19 pandemic).
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Walk-through screening station

content: Body: This standard specifies the operation of a walk-through screening station (WTSS) for mass testing as part of pandemic response management (for example, during the COVID-19 pandemic). In a WTSS, the screening process includes a medical interview, a temperature check and specimen collection. WTSS may involve a negative- or adaptable-pressure booth to help limit the spread of disease.
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Walk-through screening station

content:
The use of WTSS can reduce the risk of transmission of the disease, relieve pressure on hospitals (which otherwise can be inundated with requests for testing), and allow hospital resources to be used for disease treatment instead of screening.

Title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Drive-through screening station
Description: This standard specifies the operation of a drive-through screening station (DTSS) for mass testing as part of pandemic response management.
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Drive-through screening station

content: Body: This standard specifies the operation of a drive-through screening station (DTSS) for mass testing as part of pandemic response management. A DTSS can screen thousands of people each day. Those who test positive can be told to self-isolate or are referred for treatment, and those who had been in contact with an infected person told to self-quarantine.
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Drive-through screening station

content: People presenting for screening at a DTSS remain in their car, which acts as a protective barrier for healthcare workers. The standard protocol for operating a DTSS can include a medical interview, examination and specimen collection through the car window
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Drive-through screening station

content: . The use of DTSS can reduce the risk of transmission of the disease, relieve pressure on hospitals (which otherwise can be inundated with requests for testing), and allow hospital resources to be used for disease treatment instead of screening.
title: Healthcare organization management — Pandemic response (respiratory) — Drive-through screening station

content: Body: This standard enables the advancement of interoperability from the data/information exchange paradigm to knowledge sharing at decreasing level of abstraction, starting at IT concept level (semantic coordination) through business domain concept level (agreed service function level cooperation), domain level (cross-domain cooperation) up to individual context (skills-based end-user
title: Health informatics — Interoperability and integration reference architecture — Model and framework

content: (cross-domain cooperation) up to individual context (skills-based end-user collaboration)
title: Health informatics — Interoperability and integration reference architecture — Model and framework

content: . The standard defines a model and framework for a harmonised representation of existing or intended systems with a specific focus on ICT-supported business systems
title: Health informatics — Interoperability and integration reference architecture — Model and framework

content: . The Interoperability and integration reference architecture supports ontology harmonisation or knowledge harmonisation to enable interoperability between, and integration of, systems, standards and solutions at any level of complexity without the demand for continuously adapting/revising those specifications
title: Health informatics — Interoperability and integration reference architecture — Model and framework

content: . The approach can be used for analysing, designing, integrating and running any type of system. For realising advanced interoperability, flexible, scalable, business-controlled, adaptive, knowledge-based, intelligent health and social ecosystems need to follow a systems-oriented, architecture-centric, ontology-based and policy-driven approach.

Title: Health informatics – Development of terms and definitions for health informatics glossaries
Description: This document supports the standardisation of health informatics glossaries by setting out the requirements that terms, definitions and metadata should meet to be included in these glossaries.
title: Health informatics – Development of terms and definitions for health informatics glossaries

content: Body: Health informatics is serviced by multiple standards development organisations, each with their own need for consistent definitions of the terms they use. The evolution of glossaries across these organisations has resulted in standards in which terms are defined and/or used in different ways. This situation leads to a lack of clarity in the use and meaning of health informatics terms.
title: Health informatics – Development of terms and definitions for health informatics glossaries

content: This document addresses the need for a single international health informatics glossary. It collates relevant guidance on the development of quality terms and definitions and provides procedural standards for the introduction and management of terms, including metadata requirements
title: Health informatics – Development of terms and definitions for health informatics glossaries

content: . This guidance is intended to reduce the effort required to develop standards and support the development of international e-health initiatives.

content:

title: Healthcare organization management — Hand hygiene performance

content: Body: This standard specifies hand hygiene training, compliance benchmarking, performance/feedback, and facility requirements for healthcare facilities that operate hand hygiene systems. It covers facility readiness, hand hygiene education and training, monitoring, performance, promotion and identifying key issues that require attention for improvement
title: Healthcare organization management — Hand hygiene performance

content: . Procedures for surgical hand preparation are not addressed in this standard. Furthermore, this standard does not consider surface disinfection, instrument disinfection, ultraviolet irradiation (UV-C), heat sterilisation, room fogging or electrostatic spraying devices and any other methods typically not designed or suitable for human hand hygiene purposes
title: Healthcare organization management — Hand hygiene performance

content: . Excluding surgical hand preparation, this document applies specifically to clinical healthcare workers, patients and visitors.

Title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents
Description: This standard supports interchangeability of digital signatures and the prevention of incorrect or illegal digital signatures by providing minimum requirements and formats for generating and verifying digital signatures and related certificates.
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

content: Body: This standard supports interchangeability of digital signatures and the prevention of incorrect or illegal digital signatures by providing minimum requirements and formats for generating and verifying digital signatures and related certificates
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

content: . The standard describes the common technical, operational, and policy requirements that need to be addressed to enable digital certificates to be used in protecting the exchange of healthcare information within a single domain, between domains and across jurisdictional boundaries.
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

content: The purpose of the standard is to create a platform for global interoperability. It specifically supports digital certificate-enabled communication across borders but could also provide guidance for the national or regional deployment of digital certificates in health care
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

content: . The standard defines the provable compliance with a public key infrastructure (PKI) policy necessary in the domain of health care. It specifies a method of adopting long-term signature formats to ensure integrity and non-repudiation in long-term electronic preservation of healthcare information
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

content: . The standard provides healthcare-specific PKI (HPKI) profiles of digital signature based on the ETSI Standard and the profile of the ISO/ETSI Standard specified in CAdES, XAdES and PAdES.
title: Health informatics — Public key infrastructure — Part 4: Digital signatures for healthcare documents

Title: Health informatics – Explicit time-related expressions for healthcare-specific problems
Description: This standard specifies a set of representational primitives and semantic relations for the unambiguous representation of explicit time-related expressions in health informatics.
title: Health informatics – Explicit time-related expressions for healthcare-specific problems

content: Body: This standard specifies a set of representational primitives and semantic relations for the unambiguous representation of explicit time-related expressions in health informatics. The document provides a set of principles for syntactic and semantic representation that allow the comparability of specific ontologies on time and the exchange of explicitly expressed time-related information.
title: Health informatics – Explicit time-related expressions for healthcare-specific problems

content:
The document applies to both the representation of actual phenomena occurring in the real world (e.g. registrations in medical records) and the description of concepts (e.g. medical knowledge bases). It is not intended to be used directly for representing what is true in time, reasoning about time or representing metrological time.

content: Description: This standard facilitates the production, management and exchange of digital medical images and information by specifying protocols, command syntax and semantics, media storage services for devices and a file format and medical directory structure for interchange media.
title: Health informatics – Digital imaging and communication in medicine (DICOM) including workflow and data management

content: Body: This standard addresses the production, management and exchange of digital medical images and information
title: Health informatics – Digital imaging and communication in medicine (DICOM) including workflow and data management

content: . It facilitates the interoperability of medical imaging equipment by specifying a set of protocols for devices, the syntax and semantics of commands and associated information that can be exchanged using these protocols, a set of media storage services for devices, and a file format and medical directory structure for interchange media.
title: Health informatics – Digital imaging and communication in medicine (DICOM) including workflow and data management

content: The standard was developed with an emphasis on diagnostic medical imaging as practiced in radiology, cardiology, pathology, dentistry, ophthalmology and related disciplines, as well as image-based therapies such as interventional radiology, radiotherapy and surgery. However, it is also applicable to information exchanged in clinical, research, veterinary, and other medical environments.

Description: This standard establishes the reference standards portfolio (RSP) for the clinical imaging domain.
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

content: Body: This standard establishes the reference standards portfolio (RSP) for the clinical imaging domain. An RSP lists the principal health information technology (HIT) standards that form the basis of implementing and deploying interoperable applications in the target domain
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

content: . An RSP also includes a description of the domain, a normative list of standards, and an informative framework for mapping the standards to example deployment use cases. The lists do not include standards that are specifically national in scope.
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

content: The primary target audience for this standard is policymakers (governmental or organisational), regulators, project planners and HIT managers. This standard will also be of interest to other stakeholders such as equipment and HIT vendors, clinical and health information management (HIM) professionals and standards developers.
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

content: The intended usage of this document is to inform decisions about selecting the standards that will form the basis of integration projects in geographic regions or healthcare organisations
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

content: . This includes, for example, what standards to use for capturing/encoding/exchanging certain types of information; what standards to use for interfaces between the devices and information systems that support information capture, management, exchange, processing and use; and what standards to use for specific use cases/deployment scenarios
title: Health Informatics — Reference standards portfolio (RSP) — Clinical imaging

Title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)
Description: This document applies to the data exchange format that is designed to facilitate exchanging omics data around the world without forcing changes of any database schema. It specifies the characteristics of OML from 4 perspectives: informatics, molecular, application and biological species.
title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)

content: Body: The purpose of Omics Markup Language (OML) is to provide a standardised data exchange format for omics in human health. This document applies to the data exchange format that is designed to facilitate exchanging omics data around the world without forcing changes of any database schema
title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)

content: . It specifies the characteristics of OML from 4 perspectives: informatics, molecular, application and biological species.
title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)

content:
From an informatics perspective, OML defines the data exchange format based on XML. This document gives guidelines for the specifications of the data exchange format.
From a molecular perspective, this document applies to all types of omics data. This document is also applicable to the molecular annotations, including clinical concerns and relations with other omics concerns.
title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)

content: From an application perspective, this document applies to the clinical field including clinical practice, preventive medicine, translational research, and clinical research including drug discovery. This document does not apply to basic research and other scientific fields.
title: Genomics informatics — Omics Markup Language (OML)

content:
From a biological species perspective, this document applies to the human health-associated species, in the form of human, preclinical animals, and cell lines. This document does not apply to other biological species.

Title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 1: Reference model
Description: This document is Part 1 of a 5-part series that sets out a means for communicating about electronic health records (EHRs). Part 1 contains an Information Viewpoint specification, as defined by ISO/IEC 10746-1.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 1: Reference model

content: Body: This document is Part 1 of a 5-part series that sets out a system for communicating about electronic health records (EHRs). This system enables the communication of whole or partial EHRs between EHR systems, or between EHR systems and a centralised EHR data repository. Part 1 contains an Information Viewpoint specification, as defined by ISO/IEC 10746-1.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 1: Reference model

content: In addition to the direct communication of EHRs, the system can also be used for decision support components, personal health applications and devices that need to access or provide EHR data, or the representation of EHR data within a distributed (federated) record system.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 1: Reference model

content: This document is primarily intended to support direct care given to identifiable individuals, self-care by individuals and population monitoring systems such as disease registries and public health surveillance
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 1: Reference model

content: . It may also be useful for other purposes such as teaching, clinical audit, administration and reporting, service management, research and epidemiology, which often require the anonymisation or aggregation of individual records.

Description: This document defines a set of models collectively referred to as the Biomedical Research Integrated Domain Group (BRIDG) model. This model enables semantic interoperability within the biomedical and clinical research domain and between the research and healthcare domains.
title: Health informatics – Information models – Biomedical Research Integrated Domain Group (BRIDG) Model

content: Body: This document defines a set of models collectively referred to as the Biomedical Research Integrated Domain Group (BRIDG) model. This model supports the development of computer software, databases, metadata repositories and data interchange standards by enabling semantic interoperability within the biomedical and clinical research domain and between the research and healthcare domains
title: Health informatics – Information models – Biomedical Research Integrated Domain Group (BRIDG) Model

content: . Clinical research semantics are represented as a set of visual diagrams that describe the information relationships, definitions, explanations and examples used in protocol-driven biomedical research.
title: Health informatics – Information models – Biomedical Research Integrated Domain Group (BRIDG) Model

content:
This standard establishes the links between protocol-driven research and its associated regulatory artefacts, including the data, organisation, resources, rules, and processes involved in the formal assessment of a drug, procedure, process, subject characteristic, or device. This includes data associated with postmarketing adverse event reporting.

Description: This standard extends IEEE 11073 to support terminology for implantable cardiac devices.
title: Health informatics — Point-of-care medical device communication – Part 10103: Nomenclature — Implantable device, cardiac

content: Body: This standard extends the base nomenclature provided in IEEE 11073 to support terminology for implantable cardiac devices. It covers implantable devices such as pacemakers, defibrillators, devices for cardiac resynchronisation therapy, and implantable cardiac monitors
title: Health informatics — Point-of-care medical device communication – Part 10103: Nomenclature — Implantable device, cardiac

content: . This nomenclature defines the discrete terms necessary to convey a clinically relevant summary of the information obtained during a device interrogation.
title: Health informatics — Point-of-care medical device communication – Part 10103: Nomenclature — Implantable device, cardiac

content: Cardiology and electrophysiology practices require a central system to manage information from all vendor devices and systems. To address this requirement, the Implantable Device, Cardiac (IDC) Nomenclature defines a standard-based terminology for device data
title: Health informatics — Point-of-care medical device communication – Part 10103: Nomenclature — Implantable device, cardiac

content: . The nomenclature facilitates the transfer of data from vendor proprietary systems to the clinic electronic health record system or device clinic management system.

Title: Health informatics — Functional and structural roles
Description: This standard defines a model for expressing functional and structural roles for health professionals and consumers and populates the model with a basic set of roles for international use in health applications.
title: Health informatics — Functional and structural roles

content: Body: This standard defines a model for expressing functional and structural roles for health professionals and consumers and populates the model with a basic set of roles for international use in health applications.
title: Health informatics — Functional and structural roles

content: Functional roles (for example, a provider who is a member of a therapeutic team, attending physician, prescriber) are bound to the realisation of actions and are highly dynamic. Structural roles (e.g. licensed general practitioner, transcriptionist) are relatively static, often lasting for many years.
title: Health informatics — Functional and structural roles

content:

Title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security
Description: This document is Part 4 of a 5-part series that sets out a means for communicating about electronic health records (EHRs). Part 4 describes a methodology for specifying the privileges necessary to access EHR data.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

content: Body: This document is Part 4 of a 5-part series that sets out a system for communicating about electronic health records (EHRs). This system enables the communication of whole or partial EHRs between EHR systems, or between EHR systems and a centralised EHR data repository
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

content: . Part 4 describes a methodology for specifying the privileges necessary to access EHR data, including the representation and communication of EHR-specific information that will inform an access decision. It also refers to general security requirements that apply to EHR communications and points at technical solutions and standards that specify details on services meeting these security needs.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

content: In addition to the direct communication of EHRs, the system can also be used for decision support components, personal health applications and devices that need to access or provide EHR data, or the representation of EHR data within a distributed (federated) record system.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

content: This document is primarily intended to support direct care given to identifiable individuals, self-care by individuals and population monitoring systems such as disease registries and public health surveillance
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

content: . It may also be useful for other purposes such as teaching, clinical audit, administration and reporting, service management, research and epidemiology, which often require the anonymisation or aggregation of individual records.
title: Health informatics – Electronic health record communication – Part 4: Security

Description: This document defines the generic concepts needed to achieve continuity of care, including both the content and context of healthcare services.
title: Health informatics – System of concepts to support continuity of care

content: Body: Continuity of care is an important aspect of quality and safety in health care. Semantic interoperability is a basic requirement for continuity of care. This document defines the generic concepts needed to achieve continuity of care, including both the content and context of healthcare services.
title: Health informatics – System of concepts to support continuity of care

content: Healthcare and clinical processes reflect the interaction between a subject of care and healthcare professionals. This standard is designed to establish a common conceptual framework across national, cultural and professional barriers. It is intended to be the foundation for interoperability at all levels in healthcare organisations and for development of information systems in healthcare.

title: Health informatics – Categorial structure for terminological systems of human anatomy

Title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework
Description: This standard establishes a common health indicators conceptual framework, and aims to foster a common vocabulary and conceptual definitions for the framework. The framework defines the appropriate dimensions and sub-dimensions required to describe the health of the population and performance of a healthcare system.
title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework

content: Body: This standard establishes a common health indicators conceptual framework, and aims to foster a common vocabulary and conceptual definitions for the framework. The framework defines the appropriate dimensions and sub-dimensions required to describe the health of the population and performance of a healthcare system
title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework

content: . It is sufficiently broad (high-level) to accommodate a variety of healthcare systems, and is comprehensive, encapsulating all of the factors related to health outcomes and health system performance and utilisation, as well as regional and national variations
title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework

content: . The standard does not identify or describe individual indicators or specific data elements for the health indicators conceptual framework, nor does it address needs analysis, demand analysis or the range of activities that need to be supported for health system management. The definition of benchmarks and/or approaches used in the definition of benchmarks is outside the standard’s scope.
title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework

content: Body: This standard refers to individual case safety reports (ICSRs) for adverse drug reactions (ADR), adverse events (AE), product problems and consumer complaints that can occur from the administration or use of one or more products
title: Health informatics — Individual case safety reports (ICSRs) in pharmacovigilance – Part 1: Framework for adverse event reporting

content: . It seeks to establish an international framework for data exchange and information sharing by providing a common messaging format for transmitting ICSRs relating to these issues.
title: Health informatics — Individual case safety reports (ICSRs) in pharmacovigilance – Part 1: Framework for adverse event reporting

content: The messaging format is based on the HL7 Reference Information Model (RIM) and can be extended or constrained to accommodate a variety of reporting use cases. This standard will be harmonised with other HL7 public health and patient safety reporting standards to help ensure that messaging constructs and vocabulary are harmonised in the HL7 Public Health and Regulatory Reporting domains.
title: Health informatics — Individual case safety reports (ICSRs) in pharmacovigilance – Part 1: Framework for adverse event reporting

content:
The data elements used in this standard were identified as consistent across many of the use cases and can be applied to a variety of reporting scenarios. Specific reporting requirements within organisations or regions might vary.

Title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data
Description: This standard provides guidance on data protection requirements to facilitate the transfer of personal health data across national or jurisdictional borders.
title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data

content: Body: This standard provides guidance on data protection requirements to facilitate the transfer of personal health data across national or jurisdictional borders. It is normative only in respect of international or trans-jurisdictional exchange of personal health data
title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data

title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data

content: . The standard covers both the data protection principles that apply to international or trans-jurisdictional transfers and the security policy which an organisation adopts to ensure compliance with those principles. The standard aims to facilitate international and trans-jurisdictional health-related applications involving the transfer of personal health data
title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data

It is not intended to be a complete terminology messaging service in and of itself.
title: Health informatics — Common terminology services

content: This standard identifies the common functional characteristics that an external terminology must be able to provide. As an example, an HL7-compliant terminology service must be able to determine whether a given concept code is valid within the particular resource
title: Health informatics — Common terminology services

content: . Instead of describing a table keyed by the resource identifier and concept code, the CTS specification describes an application programming interface (API) call that takes a resource identifier and concept code as input and returns a true/false value. Each terminology developer is free to implement this API call in whatever way is most appropriate for them.

Title: CDA R2.1 (HL7 Clinical Document Architecture, Release 2.1)
Description: This standard is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of clinical documents for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients.
title: CDA R2.1 (HL7 Clinical Document Architecture, Release 2.1)

content: Body: A clinical document architecture (CDA) can contain any type of clinical content. A CDA document is a defined and complete information object that can include text, images, sounds, and other multimedia content. Typical CDA documents may include a discharge summary, imaging report, admission & physical, pathology report and more
title: CDA R2.1 (HL7 Clinical Document Architecture, Release 2.1)

content: . The most popular use is for inter-enterprise information exchange, such as is envisioned for a US Health Information Exchange (HIE).
title: CDA R2.1 (HL7 Clinical Document Architecture, Release 2.1)

content:
This standard is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of clinical documents for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients. It defines a clinical document as having the following 6 characteristics: persistence, stewardship, potential for authentication, context, wholeness, and human readability.

· CDA overview

Title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite
Description: The HL7 V3 Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7’s Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation.
title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite

content: Body: The HL7 V3 Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7’s Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides a single source that allows implementers of V3 specifications to work with the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation.
title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite

content: The V3 specification is built around subject domains that provide storyboard descriptions, trigger events, interaction designs, domain object models derived from the RIM, hierarchical message descriptors (HMDs) and a prose description of each element
title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite

content: . Implementation of these domains further depends on a non-normative V3 guide and normative specifications for data types, the XML technical specifications (ITS) or message wire format, message and control ‘wrappers’, and transport protocols.
title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite

title: HL7 Messaging Standard Version v2.7.1

content: Body: This document contains the specifications for Version 2.7.1 of the Health Level Seven Standard for electronic data exchange in healthcare environments, with special emphasis on hospitals. It serves as a way for different applications and data architectures within a similar system environment to communicate with each other
title: HL7 Messaging Standard Version v2.7.1

content: . Specifically, it addresses the interfaces of various healthcare IT systems that send or receive patient admissions/registration, discharge or transfer data, queries, resource and patient scheduling, orders, results, clinical observations, billing, master file update information, medical records, scheduling, patient referral, patient care, clinical laboratory automation, application management

content: Body: The Vital Records Domain Analysis Model states the foundational requirements for the design and development of health information exchange standards in Health Level Seven (HL7) that relate to vital records information flows (for example, reporting of fetal deaths, deaths or live births). The model facilitates consistency in the content and encoding of required vital records data

Title: HL7 Arden Syntax for Medical Logic Systems, Edition 3.0
Description: This standard defines a syntax for creating and sharing computerised health knowledge bases.
title: HL7 Arden Syntax for Medical Logic Systems, Edition 3.0

content: Body: This standard defines a syntax for creating and sharing computerised health knowledge bases. The scope of this standard is limited to knowledge bases that can be represented as a set of discrete modules, each referred to as a Medical Logic Module (MLM). Each MLM contains sufficient knowledge to make a single decision
title: HL7 Arden Syntax for Medical Logic Systems, Edition 3.0

content: . Each MLM contains sufficient knowledge to make a single decision. Clinicians, knowledge engineers, administrators and others can use the syntax to implement clinical decision support solutions to help improve quality and safety of care.
title: HL7 Arden Syntax for Medical Logic Systems, Edition 3.0

content:
The main change in this version of the standard compared with the previous version is the addition of syntax to support Health Level 7’s Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standards.

content: Title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1

content: Description: The Health Quality Measures Format provides a standard for representing a health quality measure as an electronic document. Quality measures provide an indication of an individual or organisation’s performance in relation to a specified process or outcome via the measurement of an action, process or outcome of clinical care.
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1

content: Body: A quality measure is a quantitative tool that provides an indication of an individual or organisation’s performance in relation to a specified process or outcome via the measurement of an action, process or outcome of clinical care. Quality measures are often derived from clinical guidelines and are designed to determine whether the appropriate care has been provided
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1

content: . The Health Quality Measures Format (HQMF) provides a standard for representing a health quality measure as an electronic document. Quality measures encoded in the HQMF format are referred to as electronic clinical quality measures (eCQMs).
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1

content: Title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Regulated Studies; Regulated Product Submissions (RPS), Release 2
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Regulated Studies; Regulated Product Submissions (RPS), Release 2

content: Description: This standard defines the message for exchanging information electronically between regulators and industry, and between sets of regulators. The message is designed to be flexible enough to be used to support regulatory exchanges for any regulated product (for example, foods, medical devices and medicines).
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Regulated Studies; Regulated Product Submissions (RPS), Release 2

content: Body: Regulators exchange information with regulated industries and each other to address various regulatory issues. This standard defines the message for exchanging information electronically between regulators and industry, and between sets of regulators
title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Regulated Studies; Regulated Product Submissions (RPS), Release 2

content: . The message provides the ability to describe the contents of the regulatory exchange, and all information needed to process the exchange between parties. Title: HL7 CDA R2
Description: The HL7 Version 3 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA®) is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of 'clinical documents' for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients.
title: HL7 CDA R2

content: Body: The HL7 Version 3 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA®) is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of 'clinical documents' for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients
title: HL7 CDA R2

content: . It defines a clinical document as having the following six characteristics: 1) Persistence, 2) Stewardship, 3) Potential for authentication, 4) Context, 5) Wholeness and 6) Human readability. A CDA can contain any type of clinical content - typical CDA documents would be a discharge summary, imaging report, admission and physical record, and pathology report
title: HL7 CDA R2

content: Title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

content: Description: This document provides the ‘top-level’ specification of the Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF). SAIF provides consistency between all artefacts, enables a standardised approach to developing and implementing enterprise architecture, and a way to measure the consistency.
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

content: Body: This document provides the ‘top-level’ specification of the Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF).. SAIF provides consistency between all artefacts, enables a standardised approach to developing and implementing enterprise architecture, and a way to measure the consistency. SAIF is the framework that is required to rationalise interoperability of standards
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

content: . It is an architecture for achieving interoperability, but it is not a whole-solution design for enterprise architecture management. The SAIF-CD is written for persons or organisations that are interested in implanting SAIF as an adjunct to existing (or planned) enterprise architecture frameworks
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

content: . SAIF’s singular focus is the various dimensions and perspectives that are not associated with enterprise architecture per se, but rather with achieving predictable, scalable and effective interoperability between the various software components that collectively populate one or more enterprise architectures
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

content: . Such implementations are most effectively done through the development of an organisation-specific SAIF implementation guide (SAIF IG).
title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2

title: Service Relation Instance Number (SRIN)

content: . The Application Identifier AI 8019 identifies the SRIN. Scanning the SRIN barcode enables verification that the right patient is receiving the right, electronically prescribed product at the right time. Further information is available in the GS1 General Specifications Standard.

Title: Global Model Number (GMN)
Description: This supplementary resource describes the Global Model Number (GMN), which is the GS1 key used within databases (not on products) to identify a grouped family of products or a model.
title: Global Model Number (GMN)

content: Body: This supplementary resource describes the Global Model Number (GMN), which is the GS1 key used within databases (not on products) to identify a grouped family of products or a model. The GMN enables companies to uniquely identify the product model through the entire lifecycle of the product, from design to disposal
title: Global Model Number (GMN)

content: . For example, a GMN can be used to identify products that are sold under different versions (for example, with different supporting languages), but are otherwise the same. Other examples include medical devices, for which the lifecycle can include extensive time and effort in conformity assessment before a product can be released on a market
title: Global Model Number (GMN)

content: . In the European Union (EU), the GMN supports the implementation of the requirements of the Basic Unique Device Identification and Device Identifier (UDI-DI) under the EU UDI regulations on medical devices and in-vitro diagnostics devices. The supplementary resource also includes a link to the GMN generator tool, which can be used by GS1 member organisations to create valid GMNs.
title: Global Model Number (GMN)

Companies can apply a GIAI on any asset to uniquely identify and manage that asset. This could be a computer, desk, vehicle, piece of transport equipment or spare part. Barcodes and EPC/RFID tags can be used to apply the GIAI on assets
title: Global Individual Asset Identifier (GIAI)

content: . Barcodes and EPC/RFID tags can be used to apply the GIAI on assets. Applied electronically, the GIAI enables a company to record individual assets in asset management and accounting systems across company boundaries. This document complies with the AU/ISO/IEC-1459-4, further information is available in the GS1 General Specifications Standard.

content: Body: This document specifies requests and responses for Verification of Product Identifiers, especially for pharmaceuticals. In health care, a combination of the Global Trade Item Number (GTIN) and Serial Number is often used to create a unique product instance identifier, and the Lot or Batch Number and Expiry Date are included as data attributes of that identifier
title: Lightweight Messaging Standard for Verification of Product Identifiers v1.0.2

content: . This standard is intended to provide a simple standardised lightweight messaging framework to define verification questions and corresponding responses to enable decisions to accept, reject or quarantine a product instance, based on the authentication check of the product identifier and associated data.

content: Body: This standard is a voluntary code of practice. It provides a framework for improving the positive identification of clients in health care organisations. The objective of this standard is to provide the health industry with a specific standard for health care client identification for clinical and administrative data management purposes (data structure and specification)
title: Health Care Client Identification

content: . The standard promotes uniformly good practice in identifying individuals and recording identifying data so as to ensure that each individual’s health records will be associated with that individual only. The standard also provides the basis for future linkage of data as authorised by law and appropriate for clinical management of patients and statistical research purposes.

title: GS1 Conformant Resolver — resolving GS1 identification keys to resources

content: Description: This standard defines GS1-Conformant Resolver (Release 1.1.0, Feb 2025), a protocol for translating GS1 Digital Link URIs into one or more linked resources (information or services) in a standardized and machine-discoverable way.
title: GS1 Conformant Resolver — resolving GS1 identification keys to resources

content: Body: The GS1 Conformant Resolver standard describes how resolvers should accept GS1 Digital Link URIs and return or redirect to relevant linked resources using HTTP. It supports decompression, validation, link metadata (like titles, relation types, media types), and fallback logic to ensure interoperability across distributed web systems.
title: GS1 Conformant Resolver — resolving GS1 identification keys to resources

content:
By adhering to this standard, each GS1 identifier (e.g. GTIN, GLN) can serve as a bridge between physical entities and associated digital data or services — enabling scalable, linked supply chain, product, and health information systems.
Keywords: GS1 resolver GS1 Digital Link URI resolution interoperability link metadata HTTP resolver standard

The goal of EPCIS is to enable disparate applications to create and share visibility event data, both within and across enterprises
title: EPCIS Standard v2.0

content: . This enables users to have a shared view of physical objects (for example, trade items, fixed assets, physical documents) and digital objects (for example, digital documents, electronic books), identified at a class or instance level, as each step of a business process is completed. EPCIS applies to all situations in which visibility event data is captured and shared
title: EPCIS Standard v2.0

content: . The Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules help industry make consistent decisions about how to manage the unique identification of these product based on consideration of the product, its labelling and its packaging.
title: Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules Standard v10

content: The Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules help ensure that the right product is supplied. They support operational efficiencies in the exchange of product information; the smooth operation of global supply chains; and global regulatory compliance. content: The DIMSE defines an Application Service Element (both the service and protocol) used by peer DICOM Application Entities for the purpose of exchanging medical images and related information.
